content.problemStatement:
Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are playing a fierce match of binary Nim. This novel game is played with NNN stacks, each of them containing only 111-s and 000-s.

Just like in normal Nim, Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum alternate turns; in their turn, a player must choose one non-empty stack and remove a positive number of elements from the top of this stack. However, Tweedle-Dee may only choose a stack with 000 at the top (and remove elements from it afterwards), and similarly, Tweedle-Dum may only choose a stack with 111 at the top. the player that cannot make a move loses

Suzumo does not want to wait for the end of the game, so given the starting player he asks you to determine the winner. Remember that Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are legendary grandmasters of combinatorial games, so both always play optimally.
constraints: each character in B is either '1' or '0'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains an integer N, a string S denoting the number of stacks, the player that starts the game, respectively. Each of the following N lines contains a single binary string B representing a stack; the first character of B corresponds to the top element of the stack and the last character to the bottom element.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string 'Dee' if Tweedle-Dee wins the match or 'Dum' if Tweedle-Dum wins.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the Nim sum of the number of '1's and '0's in each stack is not zero, so the first player 'Dee' will win. For the second test case, the Nim sum is zero, so the second player 'Dum' will win.
The Nim sum of the number of '1's and '0's in the stack is not zero, so the first player 'Dee' will win.
The Nim sum of the number of '1's and '0's in the stack is zero, so the second player 'Dum' will win.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of Nim theory. We can calculate the Nim sum of the number of '1's and '0's in each stack. If the Nim sum is not zero, the first player will win; otherwise, the second player will win.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * |B|) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of stacks, and |B| is the maximum length of a binary string.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * |B|) — for storing the binary strings.
